# Childhood & adolescence

> Between the high chair and adulthood, a body is quietly built to its lifetime blueprint — most
> of its bone, a decade of growth, and, just as lasting, its habits. The clinician's job shifts from
> filling gaps to *anticipating a spurt*, *guarding a closing window*, and — above all — doing no harm
> in the one stage where a careless word about weight can start real damage.

## Childhood and adolescence at a glance: the same two drivers

This chapter picks up where the *Infancy & Early Childhood* chapter leaves off — around **four to five**,
once a child eats family foods — and runs to the end of adolescence. It splits naturally into a long
**quiet phase** (school-age, ~5–10 years: steady growth, habits setting) and a short, dramatic
**adolescent spurt**. As everywhere in this part of the book, the story runs on two drivers.

- **What's changing inside** — the *internal* drivers: after years of slow, steady growth, the
  **pubertal spurt** re-accelerates height and, driven by sex hormones, splits body composition by sex;
  the **peak-bone-mass window** briefly opens and then closes for life; and **iron demand** rises with
  growth and, in girls, jumps again at menarche.
- **What's changing outside** — the *external* levers: the **school and hawker food environment**,
  **sugary drinks** and their marketing, **physical activity and sport** — and, powerfully, the handover
  of **food choice from parent to teenager and peers**.

Almost every recommendation below is one driver answering another — and, uniquely in this stage, the
biggest risks come not from a missing nutrient but from the environment and from *how we talk about
weight*. The interactive traces the one window that truly closes: tap the levers and watch the bone bank
fill.

<div data-island="bone-window" data-src="/diagrams/bone-window.yml"></div>

## The growth spurt: why "more" is the wrong mental model

After infancy, growth is slow and steady for years — then puberty re-accelerates it to a **peak height
velocity**, the fastest growth since the first year of life [@rogol_2000_growth_puberty]. The single most
useful idea here is to read a child's height as a point on a *velocity* curve, not a fixed number: the
spurt is transient, and it is timed to **pubertal stage, not birthday**. It also runs **early in girls and
late in boys** — a girl's fastest growth comes in *early* puberty, before her first period, so she is at
or near peak nutritional demand while still looking like a child; a boy's biggest spurt comes *later*, so a
boy just entering puberty still has his ahead of him [@granados_2015_phv_tanner]. Sex hormones then split
body composition: testosterone builds **lean mass** in boys, oestrogen adds **body fat** in girls — normal,
hormone-programmed physiology, not over-eating, and not something to correct [@rogol_2000_growth_puberty].

**Energy and protein needs rise — but not the way people assume.** Total daily energy climbs steeply and
splits by sex (a late-teen boy may need close to double a young child's intake), yet **per kilogram, energy
needs actually fall** through childhood, because the body grows faster than its per-kilo demand
[@faowhounu_2004_energy_children]. Protein behaves the same way: the requirement **per kilogram drifts down**
toward the adult figure, while total grams rise simply because the teenager is bigger
[@faowhounu_protein_children]. This quietly punctures the "teenagers need protein powder" narrative — an
ordinary diet that meets energy needs almost always meets protein needs too.

So what *does* explain a teenager's enormous appetite? Mostly the bigger body and the movement, not the
building work: the direct energy cost of laying down new tissue is a surprisingly small line-item — only
about **1–2% of the day's energy** by mid-adolescence [@faowhounu_2004_energy_children]. The appetite surge
is instead a **normal, hormonally coordinated signal that tracks growth velocity**
[@cheng_2018_ghrelin_puberty]. The clinical trick is to **read appetite against growth**: a mid-pubertal
teen eating far more is usually feeding a spurt, whereas intake that *doesn't* fit the growth trajectory —
restriction while the growth curve falters, or rapid fat gain — is the flag worth noticing.

**Catch-up growth narrows with age.** A young child can recover from a nutritional setback, but by later
childhood that potential shrinks, so a child crossing *down* through the growth centiles is a **refer**, not
a wait-and-see [@leroy_2020_catchup_growth]. (The reference intakes and the underlying nutrient biology
behind these needs are owned by the *Macronutrients: Carbohydrate, Protein, Fat & Fibre* and *Micronutrients,
Hydration & Electrolytes* chapters — this chapter only anticipates how they shift.)

## The peak-bone-mass window: a bank you can only fill once

Bone is not laid down evenly across life. Roughly **two-fifths of the bone mineral you will carry as an
adult is deposited in the few years around the pubertal spurt** — a brief, hormone-gated window that then
closes as the growth plates fuse [@baxterjones_2011_pbm_estimation]. This is the "bone bank," and the size
of the balance you finish adolescence with is the single biggest determinant of how much you can afford to
lose in later life — the thread the *Bone Health & Osteoporosis* and *Menopause & Midlife Health* chapters
pick up. But the window is only *partly* yours to influence: **only about a fifth to two-fifths of peak
bone mass is set by lifestyle; the rest is inherited** [@nof_weaver_2016_pbm_position]. Worth optimising —
not a lever that overrides genetics.

What actually builds the balance is more specific — and more honest — than "drink milk." The strongest
evidence is for **weight-bearing, high-impact activity and adequate calcium**; dairy and vitamin D help but
carry weaker evidence [@nof_weaver_2016_pbm_position], and exercise is **most osteogenic in early puberty**,
right when the window is widest [@hind_2007_exercise_bone_review]. Supplements are where honesty matters
most: in a child who is **already replete**, calcium supplements add only a small, fading benefit, and
vitamin D adds essentially nothing — the gains show up only in children who were **deficient** to begin
with [@winzenberg_2006_calcium_meta; @winzenberg_2011_vitd_meta]. The message is therefore **food-first
calcium, outdoor weight-bearing activity, and find-and-treat deficiency — not blanket supplementation**.

::: {.callout-warning}
## When the window is under threat: energy, not calcium, is often the problem
The fastest way to *empty* the adolescent bone bank is not too little calcium — it is too little **energy**.
In a teenager who trains hard and eats too little, the body's hormones down-shift, periods stop, and bone
accrual stalls, sometimes with lasting deficit [@ackerman_2011_athlete_bone_triad]. We meet this as the
*female-athlete triad* below; note here only that **a young athlete with irregular or absent periods, or a
stress fracture, is a bone-health emergency in slow motion — recognise it and refer**, don't reach for a
calcium tablet.
:::

## Iron and the menstruating adolescent

Iron demand rises through childhood to build an expanding blood volume and growing muscle — in *both* sexes
during the spurt — and then, in girls, **jumps again at menarche** as monthly menstrual loss stacks on top
of growth [@beard_2000_iron_adolescent_females]. This makes the **menstruating adolescent girl the classic
at-risk group**, and the risk compounds when heavy periods meet a meat-light diet — a combination that
multiplies the odds of deficiency far beyond either alone [@soderman_2025_menstrual_iron]. It is the local
teaching figure too: Singapore's iron recommendation roughly **triples for adolescent girls versus their
pre-teen years**, and sits far above boys' [@hpb_healthhub_rda].

Why it matters beyond a low haemoglobin: iron deficiency **blunts attention, memory and learning**, and
correcting it measurably improves them — though, honestly, without a clear effect on exam results
themselves [@gutema_2023_iron_cognition]. And deficiency bites **before** anaemia appears — an iron-deplete
teen can be tired and under-performing with a normal haemoglobin — which is why the earlier marker
(**ferritin**) matters more than haemoglobin alone. At this stage the competency is simple: **suspect iron
deficiency in the tired, poorly-concentrating, or heavily-menstruating adolescent — especially on a
meat-restricted diet — and check iron studies or refer**, rather than waiting for anaemia. (The exact
thresholds and screening schedules belong to clinical practice; anaemia itself sits in the *Undernutrition
& Malnutrition* chapter.)

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🍜 Getting iron from a real plate
The practical wins are about *pairing*, not supplements. **Heme iron** (in meat, fish, poultry) is far
better absorbed than the **non-heme iron** of plants, and a little meat or a vitamin-C food (a squeeze of
lime, some vegetables, fruit after the meal) can multiply absorption from a plate of rice or noodles
[@statpearls_dietary_iron; @nih_ods_iron_hp]. The common blockers are **tea and coffee** with meals — the
after-school *teh* or kopi — and calcium taken at the same time, so nudge those between meals rather than
with the iron-rich one. A vegetarian teen needs noticeably *more* dietary iron and is worth asking about
directly [@nih_ods_iron_hp].
:::

## The environment takes over: sugary drinks, marketing and the peer plate

The defining external fact of adolescence is that **food choice moves from the parent to the teenager and
their peers** — more meals out, more skipped meals, more advertising, and a weakening of the family-meal
lever just as the sugary-drink and marketing levers strengthen [@story2002adolescent]. Two environmental
forces deserve naming.

**Free sugars and sugary drinks.** The WHO ceiling — **free sugars under 10% of energy**, and better under
5% — is anchored first on **dental caries**, not weight [@who2015sugars]. On weight, the cleanest evidence
comes from trials swapping sugary for sugar-free drinks: one **double-blind trial** in children found less
weight gain over eighteen months, and an **open-label trial** in adolescents saw a benefit that had faded
by two years [@deruyter2012ssb; @ebbeling2012ssb]. The effect is real but modest, and works mostly through
**displaced calories** rather than sugar being uniquely toxic [@temorenga2012sugars]. Treat sugary drinks
as a life-stage red flag to recognise, not a poison to fear.

**Marketing and screens.** Food advertising measurably shifts what children eat and ask for
[@boyland2016adcue; @boyland2022marketing] — enough that the WHO now calls for mandatory limits on marketing
unhealthy food to under-eighteens [@who2023marketing]. Screen time tracks with heavier weight, but the
arrow runs both ways (heavier children may also choose more sedentary screen time), and much of the effect
runs *through* other levers — eating while watching, absorbing the adverts, and losing sleep — so it is best
seen as interlinked, not a single cause [@zhang2016tvobesity; @robinson2017screenmedia]. **Family meals**
are the counterweight: eating together several times a week tracks with better diets, healthier weight and
less disordered eating — an association that is largely observational and plausibly runs both ways, so it is
worth **encouraging as a low-risk, sensible habit**, not prescribing as a treatment
[@hammons2011familymeals; @berge2015familymeals].

## Sport: fuel, don't restrict

An active adolescent needs to be **fuelled, not slimmed**.

**Enough activity first.** The starting point is enough movement in the first place — the guideline is an
average of **at least 60 minutes a day** of moderate-to-vigorous activity plus muscle- and bone-strengthening
on three days a week, a target most Singaporean teens miss [@bull2020whopa; @sg24hrGuidelines2021].

**Energy availability — the key idea.** The danger at the other end is under-fuelling. Energy availability is
the energy left over for the body's own housekeeping *after* training has taken its share (intake minus the
fuel burned in exercise). When it stays too low for too long — from dieting, disordered eating, or simply not
matching a heavy training load — the body down-regulates: periods stop, bone accrual stalls, and performance
falls. This cluster is the **female-athlete triad** (low energy availability, menstrual disturbance, impaired
bone) and its broader form, **relative energy deficiency in sport** [@mountjoy2023reds; @weisskelly2016triad].

**The clinician's move.** The rule of thumb from sports medicine is that **adequate energy is the foundation,
and one part of the triad should prompt a look for the others** [@thomas2016nutritionathletic]. So an
under-fuelled adolescent with absent periods or a stress fracture needs **recognition and referral, not a
stricter diet** — detailed sports-nutrition programming belongs to the *Adulthood: working life & activity*
chapter.

## When a life-stage need tips into a clinical problem

Adolescence is where two clinical tails emerge — and, crucially, the same messages prevent **both**.

**Disordered eating — and the do-no-harm imperative.** Adolescence is the **peak onset window** for eating
disorders, with anorexia nervosa, bulimia and binge-eating disorder typically beginning in the early teens
[@swanson_2011_ncsa_ed_adolescents]. The load-bearing fact for a clinician is this: **dieting is not a
harmless habit**. In prospective studies, adolescents who diet are *far* more likely to develop a new eating
disorder — and, paradoxically, to **gain more weight** over the following years than non-dieters
[@patton_1999_adolescent_ed_onset; @neumarksztainer_2007_dieting_weightgain_eat2]. That single finding unites
both tails under one banner and dictates the safe approach, echoed by paediatric guidance: **focus on
healthy habits rather than weight, encourage family meals, and avoid all weight-talk and teasing** — the
very things that drive disordered eating [@golden_2016_aap_prevent_obesity_ed; @haines_2006_weight_teasing_eat].
How to raise weight safely — the do-no-harm interview itself — is developed in the *Assessing Diet & Talking
to Patients* chapter, which owns this skill; the task here is to **recognise and refer**.

Two things sharpen recognition. First, **eating disorders occur across the whole weight spectrum** — a
formerly heavier teenager who loses weight fast can be seriously ill, so *praising* weight loss can mask an
emerging disorder [@chew_2023_atypical_an_sg_adolescents]. Second, the recognise-level red flags are
concrete: **faltering or crossing-down growth, a marked or rapid weight change, absent periods, a stress
fracture, and purging or highly restrictive eating** — any one warrants referral, at every routine visit
[@hornberger_2021_aap_identify_ed]. Two patterns round out the picture: **ARFID** — avoidant/restrictive
eating driven by sensory aversion or fear, *without* concern about weight or shape — is where true "picky
eating" tips into the clinical, and it seams back to the *Infancy & Early Childhood* chapter
[@sanchez_arfid_2023_epi_review]; and **orthorexia**, an obsessive fixation on "clean eating," is a real and
social-media-fuelled pattern to recognise even though it is **not yet a formal diagnosis**
[@horovitz_orthorexia_2023_review].

**The other tail: obesity and emerging type-2 diabetes.** Part of what makes adolescence a high-risk window
for weight gain is ordinary physiology colliding with an obesogenic environment: **per-kilo energy needs are
falling**, and a *transient* insulin resistance is a **normal** part of puberty — so a healthy pubertal teen
carrying a little more fat is usually physiology, not disease, and should not be pathologised. The concern
is the **trajectory**: childhood obesity tends to **track into adulthood**, and paediatric type-2 diabetes
is now emerging downstream of it, including in Singapore [@singh_2008_tracking_overweight;
@nuh_paediatric_t2dm_children]. Here, too, the stage-appropriate role is to **recognise the trajectory and
its cardiometabolic risk and refer** — the management belongs to the *Obesity & Metabolic Syndrome* and
*Type 2 Diabetes* chapters, and the *do-no-harm* rule above still holds: the answer is never "go on a diet."

### When to refer — the quick list

Growth faltering or crossing *down* the centiles • suspected iron deficiency (tiredness, poor
concentration, heavy periods, a meat-restricted diet) • the under-fuelled athlete (absent periods, a stress
or recurrent fracture, dropping performance) • **any** eating-disorder red flag *across the weight spectrum*,
including rapid weight loss in a formerly heavier teen • the obesity → cardiometabolic trajectory.

## The Singapore picture

Every driver above has a local anchor — and a few local tensions worth teaching honestly.

**The school plate, and a cautionary tale.** The **Healthy Meals in Schools Programme** shapes the food a
Singaporean student meets daily: participating canteens must cut fat, sugar and salt, serve wholegrains and
vegetables, keep **plain water the default**, and discourage sugary drinks [@hpb_hmsp_page]. But the sharper
lesson is an older one. Singapore's earlier school anti-obesity scheme, **TAF ("Trim And Fit," ~1992–2007)**,
did reduce measured childhood obesity — yet it is the country's canonical example of *how not to do it*:
heavier children were singled out and teased (the acronym reads "FAT" backwards), and it was retired in 2007
for a **whole-school Holistic Health Framework** that does not stigmatise [@foo2013_sg_obesity]. A local
follow-up of anorexia-nervosa patients found that **about one in ten had been through TAF** before their
illness began — an association, not proof of cause, but a sobering one [@kuek_2015_an_singapore]. It is the
do-no-harm rule made national policy — we screen weight *without shaming the child*.

**The bubble-tea environment.** **Nutri-Grade** — the mandatory A–D drink label with an advertising ban on
the worst grade — was designed for exactly the drinks teenagers buy, and from December 2023 it extends to
**freshly-prepared beverages, bubble tea included** [@hpbNutriGrade; @mohNutriGradeFresh2023]. Its effect is
honestly **modest** — a small cut in sugar per serving, not a transformed diet [@shin2023_nutrigrade_rct] —
and local data show most children already **exceed** added-sugar guidance [@brownlee2019sgchildren], so the
label is one lever among many, best paired with the family and school environment.

**A vitamin-D tension, and a data gap.** Singapore's recommendations track the drivers well — iron roughly
tripling for adolescent girls, calcium high through the bone-building years — but set **vitamin D unusually
low**, on the assumption of abundant tropical sun [@hpb_healthhub_rda]. That assumption sits awkwardly with
regional evidence that vitamin-D insufficiency is common *despite* the sun, thanks to indoor, sun-avoidant,
screen-heavy lifestyles [@nimitphong_2013_vitd_sea] — a genuine internal-meets-external tension for an
increasingly indoor generation. Be honest about a real gap, too: direct Singapore data on *adolescent* iron
and vitamin-D status are sparse, so some teaching here leans on regional and international evidence.

**Activity and eating disorders locally.** Only about a quarter of older teens meet the daily activity
target [@tay2023sgreportcard], prompting the whole-of-government **Grow Well SG** strategy [@moh_growwellsg].
And eating disorders are neither rare nor imported: local surveys find a meaningful fraction of schoolgirls
screen at-risk (with Malay girls over-represented), presentations are **rising and diagnosed younger**, and
a large share sit at *atypical*, non-underweight presentations [@ho2006_sg_eating_risk; @wong2022_paed_ed;
@chew_2023_atypical_an_sg_adolescents] — the local face of "eating disorders span the weight spectrum."

## So — is adolescence just "feed them more"?

No — and getting that wrong is where the harm lives. The internal drivers ask for *quality and timing*, not
bulk: enough energy and iron to fuel a spurt (with the menstruating girl the one to watch), and enough
loading and calcium to fill a bone bank that **closes for good** by the early twenties. The external levers
— sugary drinks, marketing, screens, the peer plate — matter more here than at any earlier stage, and the
family meal is the quiet counterweight. And running under all of it is a rule unique to this chapter:
because **dieting and weight-talk can start real disease**, the safe posture toward any teenager's weight is
*healthy habits, never a diet* — and the skill is knowing the red flags that mean it is time to **refer**.

## Check your understanding

<div data-island="quiz" data-src="/quizzes/childhood-adolescence.quiz.yml"></div>

## Practise: the netball player who "should go on a diet"

Rachel is 14, plays competitive netball, and is brought in by her mother — who is worried Rachel has "put on
weight" and "should slim down." Rachel trains most days, her periods stopped a few months ago, and she looks
fit; her weight is in the normal range. Work through the visit — notice that the *obvious, requested* answer
is the harmful one, that a normal weight does not mean safe, and that your real job is to recognise the
pattern and refer. Your choices branch the case.

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/rachel-athlete.case.yml"></div>
